params.prompt:
Scholarly anatomical plate from a 19th-century scientific monograph, in the visual tradition of Haeckel's Kunstformen der Natur and D'Arcy Thompson's On Growth and Form. Pure crisp vector linework illustration on a perfectly flat warm cream background, no paper texture, no aging, no grain.

Centered subject: a horizontally-oriented torus, drawn in orthographic projection with a slight elevation, no glossy 3D-render shading. The torus surface is filled with a dense field of fine hairline trajectory traces — many delicate lines, each at uniform thin stroke weight, no thick bold spirals. The lines describe two interleaved families of flow:

The primary family traces slow long curves around the torus's outer circumference, like vasculature carefully drawn by a draftsman, roughly twenty such curves evenly distributed around the body.

The secondary family runs perpendicular as even finer hatching circulating across the surface, denser than the primary family, suggesting the cross-section of the surface.

Surface tonality is built from line density and engraving hatch only, not from gradient shading. No airbrush, no smooth gray fills, no glossy highlights, no 3D render look.

At one point along the torus, a Möbius-like cross-folded throat constriction: the surface visibly twists and pinches in a half-twist, accented in deep sanguine red at the constriction point. This is the ONLY red in the image — all other linework is in warm black ink.

Plate furniture: clearly defined negative-space zones reserved for typography to be composited in post-production. A horizontal blank band at the very top for plate header. A horizontal blank band at the very bottom for imprint footer. Vertical blank gutters on the far left and far right for marginalia. These zones are pure background — perfectly empty cream, no border lines, no tick marks, no markings of any kind.

Absolutely no text, no letters, no numbers, no labels, no captions, no annotations, no measurements, no hex codes, no percentages, no symbols of any kind anywhere in the image. Pure illustration only with reserved typographic negative space.

Style register: vector engraving, flat orthographic, fine hairline discipline, scholarly authority. The visual lineage is D'Arcy Thompson, Haeckel, and Vesalius. Modern execution, vector-crisp, no aging, but compositional sensibility from the 1860s scientific monograph tradition. Think the illustrated figures of a Royal Society proceedings volume.
